context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to do the Spider Diagram for my case study city, but I’m a little lost on how to organize the info into the 5 pathways. Could you help me figure out what kind of evidence belongs in each pathway?
exchanges.tutor: Great starting point! Before we dive in — what do you already know about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from the course material? Even a rough sense of what they cover would help us figure out where you're stuck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a broad orienting question about the assignment. Rather than explaining the 5 pathways for them, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge from the course. I'll ask them what they already know about the 5 pathways before scaffolding further. This keeps the cognitive work on the student's side and helps me gauge where they actually are.

exchanges.student: I only remember them kind of vaguely, and I think that’s part of my problem. I know they’re broad categories for climate action in cities, but I’m not confident about what each one actually includes. Could you give me a simple breakdown first, and then maybe I can try sorting my city info into them?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but that's actually a great reason to go back to your course materials — the 5 pathways are defined there, and reviewing them yourself will make it much easier to sort your city's evidence. Once you've looked them up, come back and tell me what you found. What do your course readings or lecture notes say about them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the 5 pathways directly, which would bypass their own learning process. Instead of giving them a breakdown, I should redirect them to their course materials — that's where the definitions live. This keeps the cognitive work on the student and is appropriate given this is foundational course content they should be reviewing. I'll be direct but not harsh.

exchanges.student: Yeah, fair. I checked, and I think they are governance, spatial planning, economic/institutional stuff, social/behavioral change, and technology/infrastructure... but I’m not totally sure I’m wording them right. I’m mostly confused about how to tell the difference between governance and planning in my city example.
exchanges.tutor: Good work going back to check! That's the right instinct. Now, think about your case study city — can you give me one concrete example of something your city is doing related to climate action? Let's use a real example to work through the distinction together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done the work of going back to their materials, which is good progress worth acknowledging. They're now asking a more focused conceptual question about distinguishing governance from planning. Rather than explaining the difference directly, I should ask them a probing question that helps them reason through the distinction themselves using their own city example. This is a good Socratic opportunity.

exchanges.student: I’m using Boston, and one example is its climate action plan / resilience strategy. My guess is that sounds like governance because it’s an official city policy thing, but part of it also affects land use and neighborhoods, so then I get stuck.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a sharp observation — one initiative can touch more than one pathway. So let me ask you this: what's the difference between *making a decision* about land use versus *changing how land is actually used*? Which side does a strategy document fall on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely good observation — a climate action plan can touch multiple pathways, and that's worth affirming. Rather than resolving the ambiguity for them, I should push them to think about what distinguishes the *act of planning/deciding* from the *physical/spatial outcomes* of that planning. A targeted question should move them toward the distinction without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I think the strategy document is more the decision-making side, so governance? And the actual zoning changes or neighborhood redesign would be spatial planning. I’m not 100% sure, but that seems like the split.
exchanges.tutor: That reasoning is solid. Now try applying that same logic to your other Boston examples — can you start sorting a few more pieces of evidence into the remaining pathways and tell me what you come up with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a solid conceptual distinction through their own reasoning. This is exactly the kind of guided discovery we want. I should affirm this without over-praising, and then push them to apply it more broadly to their spider diagram rather than dwelling further on this one distinction.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’d try it like this: the city strategy goes under governance, zoning or waterfront design changes under spatial planning, transit or building upgrades maybe under technology/infrastructure, and public outreach or community preparedness under social/behavioral. I’m still fuzzy on what exactly counts as economic/institutional, though.
exchanges.tutor: Good sorting on the other four — that shows real progress. For the economic/institutional one: think about what makes cities *financially* commit to climate action, or what *organizations and rules* enable or constrain it. Does Boston have anything like funding mechanisms, partnerships, or incentive programs related to climate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made strong progress sorting evidence across four pathways using their own reasoning. They're now stuck on the economic/institutional pathway specifically. Rather than defining it for them, I should ask a probing question that helps them think about what 'economic' and 'institutional' dimensions of climate action might look like in a city context. One well-aimed question should be enough.

exchanges.student: I think so — maybe city budget commitments, grants, public-private partnerships, or incentives for energy-efficient buildings? I’m guessing those fit because they’re about money or the institutions making implementation possible. Is that basically the right idea?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that reasoning holds up well. You now have a working sense of all 5 pathways and some Boston examples for each. At this point, the most useful next step is to start filling in your actual spider diagram table. Give it a try and come back if you get stuck on a specific entry.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned their way to a solid understanding of the economic/institutional pathway on their own. I should affirm this briefly and then push them toward actually writing up their spider diagram, since we've now covered all 5 pathways and the conversation risks becoming circular if we keep discussing concepts without the student producing anything.

exchanges.student: Okay, that helps a lot. Before I fill it in, could you quickly check if I’m supposed to put one example per pathway or if it makes more sense to list a few short pieces of evidence under each?
exchanges.tutor: That's worth checking in the assignment instructions directly — they'll be the most reliable source. From what I can see in the assignment overview, it doesn't specify a fixed number. What do *you* think would give a more complete picture of your city's climate action?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a question about assignment format/expectations. I should refer them to the assignment description, which doesn't specify a number of examples per pathway. Rather than guessing or over-scaffolding, I should redirect them to the assignment instructions and encourage them to use their judgment, since this is a minor formatting question that shouldn't delay them from starting.

exchanges.student: I think a few short pieces of evidence under each would make more sense, as long as I keep it concise. I just don’t want to overdo it or make the table messy.
exchanges.tutor: That sounds like a sensible approach. Trust your judgment on that — now go fill it in. You have what you need to get started.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has answered their own question with reasonable judgment. I should briefly affirm and redirect them to actually start filling in the table, as further discussion of this formatting question would be an unnecessary detour. We're at exchange 9 of 10, so I should encourage them to move forward with the work.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll do that. If I end up with a pathway where Boston has overlapping examples, I’ll probably just note the strongest fit and maybe mention the overlap briefly.
exchanges.tutor: That's a thoughtful approach — noting the strongest fit and flagging overlap briefly is exactly the kind of critical thinking the assignment rewards. Go fill it in, and come back if you hit a specific snag.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and showing good independent judgment about handling overlapping examples. This is a positive sign. I should briefly affirm their approach and close the conversation productively, encouraging them to return if they hit a specific snag while filling in the table.
